title: Why Cash-Flowing Rentals Beat Appreciation Bets
excerpt: A quick take on why investors chasing appreciation often underperform those who prioritize monthly cash flow, and how to run the numbers before you buy.
fullText:
Most new real estate investors get seduced by appreciation stories — the property that doubled in five years, the neighborhood that "is about to pop." But appreciation is a bet on the market, not a plan. Cash flow is what pays your mortgage, covers repairs, and lets you sleep at night when prices dip.

Here's the simple test: before you buy, run the numbers assuming zero appreciation for ten years. If the deal still pencils — rent covers mortgage, taxes, insurance, maintenance reserve, and vacancy, with something left over — it's a good deal regardless of what happens to price. If it only works because you're assuming 5% annual appreciation, you're not investing, you're speculating.

This doesn't mean ignore appreciation. Markets with strong job growth and limited new supply tend to appreciate for real, structural reasons. But treat it as upside, not the thesis. Underwrite the boring case first.
